are a standard set of [Chinese character](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") forms used to [write Chinese languages](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese"). In [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), the set of traditional characters is regulated by the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Education (Taiwan)") and standardized in the *[Standard Form of National Characters](/wiki/Standard_Form_of_National_Characters "Standard Form of National Characters")*. These forms were predominant in written Chinese until the middle of the 20th century,[[1]](#cite_note-BiWei-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) when various [countries that use Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_family_of_scripts "Chinese family of scripts") began standardizing simplified sets of characters, often with characters that existed before as well-known [variants](/wiki/Variant_Chinese_characters "Variant Chinese characters") of the predominant forms.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

**Traditional Chinese characters** are a standard set of [Chinese character](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") forms used to [write Chinese languages](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese"). In [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), the set of traditional characters is regulated by the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Education (Taiwan)") and standardized in the *[Standard Form of National Characters](/wiki/Standard_Form_of_National_Characters "Standard Form of National Characters")*. These forms were predominant in written Chinese until the middle of the 20th century,[[1]](#cite_note-BiWei-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) when various [countries that use Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_family_of_scripts "Chinese family of scripts") began standardizing simplified sets of characters, often with characters that existed before as well-known [variants](/wiki/Variant_Chinese_characters "Variant Chinese characters") of the predominant forms.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[Simplified characters](/wiki/Simplified_characters "Simplified characters") as codified by the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") are predominantly used in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China"), Malaysia, and Singapore. "Traditional" as such is a [retronym](/wiki/Retronym "Retronym") applied to non-simplified character sets in the wake of widespread use of simplified characters. Traditional characters are commonly used in [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), as well as in most [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") communities outside of Southeast Asia.[[5]](#cite_note-5) As for non-Chinese languages written using Chinese characters, Japanese [kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") include many simplified characters known as *[shinjitai](/wiki/Shinjitai "Shinjitai")* standardized after World War II, [sometimes distinct from their simplified Chinese counterparts](/wiki/Differences_between_Shinjitai_and_Simplified_characters "Differences between Shinjitai and Simplified characters"). Korean [hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja"), still used to a certain extent in [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"), remain virtually identical to traditional characters, with variations between the two forms largely stylistic.

There has historically been a [debate on traditional and simplified Chinese characters](/wiki/Debate_on_traditional_and_simplified_Chinese_characters "Debate on traditional and simplified Chinese characters").[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) Because the simplifications are fairly systematic, it is possible to convert computer-encoded characters between the two sets, with the main issue being [ambiguities in simplified representations](/wiki/Ambiguities_in_Chinese_character_simplification "Ambiguities in Chinese character simplification") resulting from the merging of previously distinct character forms. Many Chinese online newspapers allow users to switch between these character sets.[[8]](#cite_note-yzzk-8)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

Traditional characters are known by different names throughout the Chinese-speaking world. The government of Taiwan officially refers to traditional Chinese characters as 正體字; 正体字; *zhèngtǐzì*; 'orthodox characters'.[[9]](#cite_note-9) This term is also used outside Taiwan to distinguish standard characters, including both simplified, and traditional, from other [variants and idiomatic characters](/wiki/Variant_Chinese_characters "Variant Chinese characters").[[10]](#cite_note-10) Users of traditional characters elsewhere, as well as those using simplified characters, call traditional characters 繁體字; 繁体字; *fántǐzì*; 'complex characters', 老字; *lǎozì*; 'old characters', or 全體字; 全体字; *quántǐzì*; 'full characters' to distinguish them from simplified characters.

Some argue that since traditional characters are often the original standard forms, they should not be called "complex". Conversely, there is a common objection to the description of traditional characters as "standard", due to their not being used by a large population of Chinese speakers. Additionally, as the process of Chinese character creation often made many characters more elaborate over time, there is sometimes a hesitation to characterize them as "traditional".[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Some people refer to traditional characters as "proper characters" (正字; *zhèngzì* or 正寫; *zhèngxiě*) and to simplified characters as 簡筆字; 简笔字; *jiǎnbǐzì*; 'simplified-stroke characters' or 減筆字; 减笔字; *jiǎnbǐzì*; 'reduced-stroke characters', as the words for *simplified* and *reduced* are homophonous in [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"), both pronounced as *jiǎn*.

## Use by region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Use by region")]

Further information: [Written Chinese § Evolution](/wiki/Written_Chinese#Evolution "Written Chinese")

The modern shapes of traditional Chinese characters first appeared with the emergence of the [clerical script](/wiki/Clerical_script "Clerical script") during the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") c. 200 BCE, with the sets of forms and norms more or less stable since the [Southern and Northern dynasties](/wiki/Southern_and_Northern_dynasties "Southern and Northern dynasties") period c. the 5th century.

### Mainland China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Mainland China")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/%E5%BB%A3%E5%B7%9E%E6%97%A5%E5%A0%B1%E5%8D%B0%E5%8B%99%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83.JPG/250px-%E5%BB%A3%E5%B7%9E%E6%97%A5%E5%A0%B1%E5%8D%B0%E5%8B%99%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E5%BB%A3%E5%B7%9E%E6%97%A5%E5%A0%B1%E5%8D%B0%E5%8B%99%E4%B8%AD%E5%BF%83.JPG)

The *[Guangzhou Daily](/wiki/Guangzhou_Daily "Guangzhou Daily")*, an official Communist Party newspaper, uses traditional Chinese characters in its branding.

Although the majority of Chinese text in mainland China are [simplified characters](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"), there is no legislation prohibiting the use of traditional Chinese characters, and often traditional Chinese characters remain in use for stylistic and commercial purposes, such as in shopfront displays and advertising.[[12]](#cite_note-etaiwannews-12) Traditional Chinese characters remain ubiquitous on buildings that predate the promulgation of the current simplification scheme, such as former government buildings, religious buildings, educational institutions, and historical monuments. Traditional Chinese characters continue to be used for ceremonial, cultural, scholarly/academic research, and artistic/decorative purposes.[[12]](#cite_note-etaiwannews-12)

In the People's Republic of China, traditional Chinese characters are standardized according to the [Table of Comparison between Standard, Traditional and Variant Chinese Characters](/wiki/Table_of_Comparison_between_Standard,_Traditional_and_Variant_Chinese_Characters "Table of Comparison between Standard, Traditional and Variant Chinese Characters").[[13]](#cite_note-tygfhzb-13) Dictionaries published in mainland China generally show both simplified and their traditional counterparts.[[14]](#cite_note-14) There are differences between the accepted traditional forms in mainland China and elsewhere, for example the accepted traditional form of 产 in mainland China is 産 (also the accepted form in Japan and Korea), while in Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan the accepted form is 產 (also the accepted form in Vietnamese [chữ Nôm](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m "Chữ Nôm")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]).[[15]](#cite_note-tyhzzxb-15)

The PRC tends to print material intended for people in Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan, and overseas Chinese in traditional characters. For example, versions of the *[People's Daily](/wiki/People%27s_Daily "People's Daily")* are printed in traditional characters, and both *People's Daily* and *[Xinhua](/wiki/Xinhua "Xinhua")* have traditional character versions of their website available, using [Big5](/wiki/Big5 "Big5") encoding.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) Mainland companies selling products in Hong Kong, Macau and Taiwan use traditional characters in order to communicate with consumers; the inverse is equally true as well. In digital media, many cultural phenomena imported from Hong Kong and Taiwan into mainland China, such as music videos, karaoke videos, subtitled movies, and subtitled dramas, use traditional Chinese characters.

### Hong Kong and Macau

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hong Kong and Macau")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Postscript-viewer-blue.svg) | This article **needs attention from an expert in China**. The specific problem is: **The differences between traditional characters as used in Taiwan versus in Hong Kong.** [WikiProject China](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_China "Wikipedia:WikiProject China") may be able to help recruit an expert. *(September 2023)* |

In [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") and [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau"), traditional characters were retained during the colonial period, while the mainland adopted simplified characters. Simplified characters are contemporaneously used to accommodate immigrants and tourists, often from the mainland.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The increasing use of simplified characters has led to concern among residents regarding protecting what they see as their local heritage.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Taiwan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Taiwan")]

[Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") has never adopted simplified characters. The use of simplified characters in government documents and educational settings is discouraged by the government of Taiwan.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Nevertheless, with sufficient context simplified characters are likely to be successfully read by those used to traditional characters, especially given some previous exposure. Many simplified characters were previously variants that had long been in some use, with systematic stroke simplifications used in folk handwriting since antiquity.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Singapore")]

Traditional characters were recognized as the official script in Singapore until 1969, when the government officially adopted Simplified characters.[[27]](#cite_note-reflection_on_use_of_simplified_Chinese_in_Singapore-27) Traditional characters still are widely used in contexts such as in baby and corporation names, advertisements, decorations, official documents and in newspapers.[[8]](#cite_note-yzzk-8)

### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Philippines")]

The [Chinese Filipino](/wiki/Chinese_Filipino "Chinese Filipino") community continues to be one of the most conservative in Southeast Asia regarding simplification.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Although major public universities teach in simplified characters, many well-established Chinese schools still use traditional characters. Publications such as the *[Chinese Commercial News](/wiki/Chinese_Commercial_News "Chinese Commercial News")*, *[World News](/wiki/World_News_(newspaper) "World News (newspaper)")*, and *[United Daily News](/wiki/United_Daily_News_(Philippines) "United Daily News (Philippines)")* all use traditional characters, as do some Hong Kong–based magazines such as *[Yazhou Zhoukan](/wiki/Yazhou_Zhoukan "Yazhou Zhoukan")*. The *Philippine Chinese Daily* uses simplified characters.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Announcement_in_a_Filipino_Chinese_daily_newspaper_%28Traditional_Chinese%29.jpg/250px-Announcement_in_a_Filipino_Chinese_daily_newspaper_%28Traditional_Chinese%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Announcement_in_a_Filipino_Chinese_daily_newspaper_(Traditional_Chinese).jpg)

Advertisement in a Filipino Chinese daily newspaper written in traditional Chinese characters

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: North America")]

With most having immigrated to the United States during the second half of the 19th century, Chinese Americans have long used traditional characters. When not providing both, US public notices and signs in Chinese are generally written in traditional characters, more often than in simplified characters.[[28]](#cite_note-:0-28)

## Use on computers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Use on computers")]

### Encoding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Encoding")]

Main article: [Chinese character encoding](/wiki/Chinese_character_encoding "Chinese character encoding")

See also: [Han unification](/wiki/Han_unification "Han unification")

In the past, traditional Chinese was most often encoded on computers using the [Big5](/wiki/Big5 "Big5") standard, which favored traditional characters. However, the ubiquitous [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") standard gives equal weight to simplified and traditional Chinese characters, and has become by far the most popular encoding for Chinese-language text.

### Input methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Input methods")]

Main article: [Chinese input methods](/wiki/Chinese_input_methods "Chinese input methods")

There are various [input method editors](/wiki/Input_method_editors "Input method editors") (IMEs) available for the [input of Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_input_methods_for_computers "Chinese input methods for computers"). Many characters, often [dialectical](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese") variants, are encoded in Unicode but cannot be inputted using certain IMEs, with one example being the [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese")-language character U+20C8E 𠲎 CJK UNIFIED IDEOGRAPH-20C8E—a composition of 伐 with the ⼝ 'MOUTH' radical—used instead of the Standard Chinese 嗎; 吗.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Typefaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Typefaces")]

Further information: [List of CJK fonts](/wiki/List_of_CJK_fonts "List of CJK fonts")

[Typefaces](/wiki/Typefaces "Typefaces") often use the initialism `TC` to signify the use of traditional Chinese characters, as well as `SC` for [simplified Chinese characters](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"). In addition, the [Noto](/wiki/Noto_fonts "Noto fonts") family of typefaces, for example, also provides separate fonts for the traditional character set used in Taiwan (`TC`) and the set used in Hong Kong (`HK`).[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Webpages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Webpages")]

Most Chinese-language webpages now use Unicode for their text. The [World Wide Web Consortium](/wiki/World_Wide_Web_Consortium "World Wide Web Consortium") (W3C) recommends the use of the [language tag](/wiki/Language_tag "Language tag") `zh-Hant` to specify webpage content written with traditional characters.[[30]](#cite_note-30)
[ISO 15924](/wiki/ISO_15924 "ISO 15924") offers `Hant` to mark text in traditional Chinese and `Hntl`[[31]](#cite_note-IETF-31) for text mixing traditional Chinese and Latin characters, sometimes used for [Taiwanese Hokkien](/wiki/Pe%CC%8Dh-%C5%8De-j%C4%AB#Han-Romanization_mixed_script "Pe̍h-ōe-jī").

## Comparison with other scripts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Traditional_Chinese_characters&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Comparison with other scripts")]

In the [Japanese writing system](/wiki/Japanese_writing_system "Japanese writing system"), *[kyujitai](/wiki/Kyujitai "Kyujitai")* are traditional forms, which were simplified to create *[shinjitai](/wiki/Shinjitai "Shinjitai")* for standardized Japanese use following World War II. Kyūjitai are mostly congruent with the traditional characters in Chinese, save for minor stylistic variation. Characters that are not included in the *[jōyō kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji")* list are generally recommended to be printed in their traditional forms, with a few exceptions. Additionally, there are *[kokuji](/wiki/Kokuji "Kokuji")*, which are kanji wholly created in Japan, rather than originally being borrowed from China.

In the [Korean writing system](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"), [hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja")—replaced almost entirely by [hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul") in [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") and totally replaced in [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea")—are mostly identical with their traditional counterparts, save minor stylistic variations. As with Japanese, there are autochthonous hanja, known as *[gukja](/wiki/Gukja "Gukja")*.

Traditional Chinese characters are also used by non-Chinese ethnic groups. The [Maniq people](/wiki/Maniq_people "Maniq people") living in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") and [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") use Chinese characters to write the [Kensiu language](/wiki/Kensiu_language "Kensiu language").[[32]](#cite_note-Glossary_of_Aslian_Languages.-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)
